chunk_summary: Cholestasis causes fat and fat-soluble vitamin malabsorption, requiring individualized supplementation, energy-dense nutrition, and structured follow-up to address deficiencies and maintain essential fatty acid balance.
learning_objectives: Understand the impact of cholestasis on fat and fat-soluble vitamin absorption.
Learn appropriate dosing strategies for fat-soluble vitamin replacement in pediatric cholestasis.
Recognize clinical signs of malabsorption and implement tailored nutrition interventions.
Explore the role of MCT and essential fatty acid management in cholestasis.
key_facts: Cholestasis impairs bile acid–dependent micelle formation, leading to fat malabsorption.
Vitamin A replacement: 5,000 units/day (<10 kg) or 10,000 units/day (>10 kg); up to 25,000 units/day for severe deficiency.
Vitamin D dosing: 2,000–5,000 units/day for low levels (8–<20 ng/mL); higher doses for severe deficiency with monitoring.
Vitamin E (TPGS): 15–25 IU/kg/day; 25 IU/kg/day for deficiency treatment.
Vitamin K: 2–5 mg/day, with parenteral replacement when clinically indicated.
MCT is absorbed more directly than long-chain fats and can be used as a calorie source.
reference_quote: ‘Standard multivitamins’ are often inadequate; individualized dosing and monitoring are required. (NASPGHAN position paper: Nutrition support of children with chronic liver disease)
document_key_topics: Nutrition interventions for essential fatty acid balance
Clinical signs and management of malabsorption
